Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Michael Bratman

b. 1945

Planning agency, self-governance, and the temporally extended structure of intention.

Michael Bratman is not, in the ordinary taxonomy of contemporary philosophy, a philosopher of “the soul” or of phenomenal consciousness. He is a philosopher of action, intention, planning agency, practical rationality, self-governance, shared agency, and institutional agency. That matters because the right report is not: “Bratman has a doctrine of the soul.” He does not appear to. The right report is: Bratman gives a powerful, systematic account of the practical mind: the mind as organized by intentions, plans, policies, and norms of diachronic and social coordination. From that, one can extract a philosophy of mind in which “soul,” if the term is admitted, names not an immaterial inner substance but the structured, temporally extended, self-governing economy of will.

The most compact thesis is this: Bratman naturalizes the practical soul without trivializing it. He rejects the idea of a separate inner ruler, a homuncular self standing outside the psychological economy and pulling the strings. But he also refuses to reduce human agency to momentary desire-belief causation. The minded person is, for him, a planning agent: a being whose intentions and plans settle practical questions, organize conduct over time, sustain self-governance, and make forms of shared and institutional life possible. His philosophy of mind is therefore practical, diachronic, anti-homuncular, broadly naturalistic, and socially extendable, but not eliminative about agency.

1. Orientation: where Bratman’s project sits

Bratman’s central research areas are philosophy of action, social and institutional organization and agency, practical rationality, and individual agency over time. His major books include Intention, Plans, and Practical Reason; Faces of Intention; Structures of Agency; Shared Agency; Planning, Time, and Self-Governance; and Shared and Institutional Agency. Stanford’s own profile describes his work in exactly this orbit: action, practical reason, individual agency over time, and social/institutional agency.

That bibliographic fact already tells us something crucial. Bratman’s “mind” is not primarily the perceptual mind, the phenomenal mind, or the Cartesian mind. It is the mind as agentive: the mind insofar as it acts, decides, commits, plans, governs itself, resists temptation, coordinates with others, and participates in institutions. His question is not “What is qualia?” but “What conception of mind and rational agency emerges once future-directed intentions and plans are treated as central inputs into practical reasoning?” The summary of Intention, Plans, and Practical Reason puts the point directly: the book asks what happens to our conception of mind and rational agency when future-directed intentions and plans are taken seriously as inputs into later practical reasoning.

This makes Bratman especially important for any philosophy of mind that cares about will, personhood, autonomy, moral responsibility, shared action, promise, institutional life, or religious practice. It makes him less directly useful for theories of raw sensation, perception, neural correlates of consciousness, or the metaphysics of an immortal soul. The report should therefore keep two levels distinct: first, Bratman’s explicit theory; second, the philosophy of mind and soul that can be reconstructed from it.

2. The basic Bratmanian move: beyond desire-belief psychology

Bratman’s foundational claim is that intentions are not reducible to desires and beliefs. He presents human beings as “planning creatures”: we settle on plans in advance, and those plans guide later conduct, coordinate activity over time, and coordinate activity with others in ways that mere desires and beliefs do not. In “Two Faces of Intention,” he explicitly says that intentions are distinctive states of mind and that they should not be reduced to ordinary desires and beliefs.

This is the decisive point for philosophy of mind. A simple desire-belief model says, roughly: an agent acts because she wants some outcome and believes some action will help bring it about. Bratman’s model says this is incomplete. Human practical mentality also includes commitments that settle issues for future action. These commitments do not merely register what one wants most at a given moment. They structure what will count as a relevant practical question later.

For example, once I intend to attend a conference tomorrow, I need not reconsider from scratch whether to attend every time a new desire appears. I now deliberate about booking transport, preparing notes, waking up on time, and declining incompatible invitations. The intention changes the architecture of future reasoning. It does not just add another desire to a motivational pile.

The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy’s entry on intention situates Bratman in this way: he emphasized commitment as central to intention, and intention is not merely predominant desire. This is why Bratman’s theory has consequences for the philosophy of mind. It implies that a central class of mental states is not best understood as passive representation or occurrent conscious episode, but as practical commitment with organizing force.

3. Intentions as partial plans

The key Bratmanian mental state is intention, and the key structure is the plan. Bratman’s planning theory treats intentions as stable, partial plans concerning present or future conduct. A Cambridge description of Faces of Intention summarizes the view this way: when one settles on a plan of action, one commits to future conduct in ways that support coordination and organization both within one agent and among multiple agents.

The word “partial” is important. A plan is normally incomplete. I may intend to write a book without yet knowing the final chapter titles, the precise daily schedule, or every argument. The plan is not a complete script. But it does constrain later deliberation. It creates means-end questions: How shall I write it? When? With what materials? In what order? It also excludes incompatible paths, at least provisionally.

This yields a picture of the mind as structured but revisable. Plans are neither rigid programs nor passing impulses. They have stability, but they can be reconsidered. They are settled enough to guide conduct, but incomplete enough to be filled in as circumstances change. That is a subtle and realistic model of practical mentality.

The joint paper by Bratman, Israel, and Pollack on resource-bounded practical reasoning makes this explicit in a quasi-architectural idiom. It presents plans as devices that constrain further practical reasoning, making deliberation tractable for finite agents. In their BDI-style framework—belief, desire, intention—intentions are organized into larger plans and play roles in filtering options, driving means-end reasoning, and influencing what the agent treats as settled.

That matters deeply for mind and consciousness. Bratman’s planning mind is not omniscient, infinitely deliberative, or purely calculative. It is finite. It has limited cognitive resources. It therefore needs structures that reduce deliberative burden. Planning is not a luxury added to rationality; it is a condition for human-scale rational agency.

4. The mind as a resource-bounded practical architecture

Bratman’s philosophy of mind is architectural. He does not merely list mental states; he explains their functional interrelations. Beliefs represent how things are. Desires represent attractive possibilities or motivational pulls. Intentions and plans settle practical questions, constrain later deliberation, trigger means-end reasoning, and coordinate action across time.

The resource-bounded character of this architecture is central. Bratman, Israel, and Pollack write from the standpoint of agents who cannot deliberate from scratch about everything all the time. Existing plans narrow the field of relevant options and allow the agent to reason within a structured background rather than in an indefinitely open space. Once one has formed a plan, subsequent reasoning often proceeds by figuring out how to implement it, how to preserve consistency with it, and how to fill it in.

A planning agent therefore lives inside a deliberative ecology shaped by prior commitments. This is one of Bratman’s strongest contributions to philosophy of mind: the mind is not merely a container of current mental episodes. It is a temporally structured system whose prior commitments configure later attention, deliberation, and action.

This also prevents an overly intellectualist picture. Planning is rational, but it is not pure calculation. It is a way of being rational under limits. It allows agents to avoid obsessive reconsideration, coordinate with their future selves, coordinate with others, and build larger projects. Bratman’s “rational planning agency” is thus a theory of bounded rationality, not an idealized omniscient decision theory. A Cambridge discussion of rational planning agency says that prior partial plans settle practical questions, pose means-end problems, filter solutions, guide action, and organize life over time and socially in a way compatible with resource limits.

5. The ontology of Bratmanian mental states

Bratman’s view is not reductive behaviorism. Intentions are real mental states. But they are not mysterious Cartesian items either. Their identity is largely given by their role in practical reasoning and action. They commit, guide, constrain, organize, and rationally pressure the agent toward coherence.

This makes Bratman’s philosophy of mind broadly functionalist in spirit, though not necessarily in the narrow computationalist sense. An intention is what it is partly because of what it does in the practical economy. It has a characteristic role: it settles a matter for the agent, supports means-end reasoning, resists easy abandonment, and coordinates conduct over time.

This is why Bratman is important for the “soul” question. Traditional accounts of the soul often identify the soul with the seat of will, rational direction, self-command, and responsible action. Bratman relocates many of those functions inside a naturalistic architecture of planning states. Lumer’s discussion of intentions notes that Bratman popularized a functional view on which intentions are executive mental states that represent actions and tend to cause, guide, and sustain their execution; Lumer contrasts this with an older tradition, including Locke, in which volitions were treated as exertions of a soul’s power to initiate, continue, or stop bodily movement.

So Bratman does not deny that there is something like will. He redescribes will in terms of intention-like commitments and practical organization. In Structures of Agency, he connects will to intention-like commitments to action and to modes of practical reasoning, while resisting the idea that agential authority is grounded simply in evaluative judgment.

6. Bratman’s “soul,” reconstructed: no separate substance, but a practical unity

There is no evidence that Bratman develops a theological or metaphysical doctrine of the soul. The closest Bratmanian analogue of soul is not an immaterial substance but the organized “psychic economy” of a planning agent. This is especially clear in his work on self-governance over time. He explicitly says that self-governance should not be understood as the work of a separate self standing outside the psychological economy and pulling the strings. Instead, he seeks a model of cross-temporal psychic functioning that constitutes self-governance.

That anti-homuncular claim is crucial. Bratman rejects the picture of a little ruler inside the agent. There is no inner monarch, ghostly ego, or separate soul-substance doing the governing. But he also does not collapse the person into a bundle of momentary impulses. Self-governance emerges from the way certain plan states, policies, and practical standpoints organize the agent’s action over time.

A Bratmanian “soul,” if one insists on using that term, would be the agent’s diachronic practical unity: the structured organization of commitments, policies, intentions, and higher-order practical attitudes through which a person governs action and thought over time. It is not separate from the psychological economy. It is a form of organization within that economy.

This is a subtle alternative to both Cartesian dualism and reductive eliminativism. The agent is not an immaterial controller. But neither is the agent merely a current desire, a neural event, or a passive stream of experiences. The agent is an organized planning system whose commitments have real authority in practical life.

7. Self-governance and the practical standpoint

Bratman’s account of self-governance is one of the main places where his theory becomes a philosophy of personhood. Self-governance involves guidance by a relevant practical standpoint. That standpoint helps determine where the agent stands, what she treats as settled, and how she organizes later conduct. Bratman emphasizes that this does not require appeal to an irreducible little person in the head.

This gives Bratman a distinctive theory of selfhood. The self is not a metaphysical atom behind mental life. Nor is it merely a narrative imposed after the fact. It is a practical standpoint embedded in a structure of planning attitudes. That standpoint can guide action, resist temptation, and organize temporally extended activity.

In Structures of Agency, Bratman links self-governance to strong forms of human agency such as self-determination and autonomy. He argues that we can understand self-governed agency by reflecting on how planning attitudes organize action and practical thought across time.

The Italian reception of Bratman rightly stresses this point. Ginocchietti describes Bratman’s work as concerned with strong reflectiveness, planning agency, temporally extended agency, autonomy, and self-determination; he also notes Bratman’s effort to defend the role of the agent within a causal theory of action. Verrucci similarly presents Bratman’s planning theory as a way of understanding agency constitution, self-governing strategies, and personal continuity over time, especially against pressures from cognitive neuroscience, social psychology, and decision theory.

That Italian emphasis is helpful because it shows a reception of Bratman not merely as a technical theorist of intention, but as a philosopher of agency under modern pressure: the pressure to explain how persons remain agents if action is causally embedded, socially influenced, and psychologically bounded.

8. Diachronic mind: the person as extended across time

A central Bratmanian idea is that agency is temporally extended. Human beings do not merely act in isolated moments. We start projects, continue them, revise them, resist interruptions, coordinate with future stages of ourselves, and bind present conduct to future commitments.

In “A Planning Agent’s Self-Governance Over Time,” Bratman says that almost all intentional activity is embedded in planned, temporally extended activity structured by prior partial plans. Those plans settle practical issues, pose problems of means, filter solutions, guide action, and support cross-temporal and social organization compatible with human cognitive and epistemic limits.

This is one of the deepest points for philosophy of mind. Much contemporary philosophy of mind is synchronic: it asks what mental state the subject is in now. Bratman’s model is diachronic: it asks how mental states organize the agent across time. The self is not just a present consciousness; it is a continuing practical organization.

Bratman connects this to Lockean themes of personal identity. Plans can cross-refer, and patterns of intention and planning can induce psychological connectedness and continuity. He does not simply identify personal identity with memory, as in simplified Locke-inspired views. Rather, he emphasizes practical continuity: the way earlier plan states shape later deliberation and action.

This has theological significance. Many religious accounts of the soul are concerned not only with consciousness but with steadfastness, conversion, repentance, vow, fidelity, temptation, habit, and character over time. Bratman provides a secular anatomy of these phenomena. He gives conceptual tools for analyzing how a person remains practically one across time, how commitments survive pressure, and how future-directed agency can be stable without being frozen.

9. Willpower, temptation, and weakness

Bratman’s theory is especially illuminating about temptation and willpower. If an intention is a commitment embedded in a plan, then temptation is not merely a stronger desire appearing at a later time. It is a disruption in the diachronic organization of agency. A temptation threatens to reopen what had been settled, to fragment the agent’s practical standpoint, or to induce local reasoning that betrays a larger project.

Bratman’s work on diachronic self-governance explicitly treats planning as a way of responding to threats such as temptation and procrastination. The end of diachronic self-governance helps stabilize agency over time and supports willpower, while also allowing that prior projects may sometimes collapse in light of new projects or changed standpoints.

This is not mere stoicism or rigidity. A rational planning agent must sometimes reconsider. Blind persistence can be irrational. The Bratmanian question is not “How can the agent never change?” but “How can the agent distinguish rational reconsideration from shuffling, weakness, procrastination, or fragmentation?” That distinction is central to any serious philosophy of will.

Here Bratman also speaks to theology indirectly. In many theological vocabularies, sin, temptation, conversion, discipline, and sanctification are diachronic phenomena. They concern the stability or corruption of the will over time. Bratman does not supply theological content—he does not say which ends are holy, sinful, virtuous, or vicious—but he gives a formal account of how practical unity and disunity work.

A religiously informed Bratmanian might say: temptation threatens not merely belief or desire but the integrity of the planning soul. A vow, discipline, or rule of life helps preserve a practical standpoint across time. Repentance is not just a new feeling but a reorganization of policies and plans. Grace, if one gives it a theological interpretation, would have to affect not only momentary desire but the agent’s deep structure of practical governance.

10. Consciousness: Bratman’s limited but important contribution

Bratman is not a theorist of phenomenal consciousness. He does not offer an account of qualia, subjective feel, perceptual awareness, or the hard problem. If one asks, “Why is there something it is like to be a conscious subject?” Bratman is not the philosopher to whom one should primarily turn.

But Bratman does contribute to a different question: what is practical consciousness? That is, what is it for an agent to know where she stands, to act from a standpoint, to guide herself by commitments, and to take responsibility for temporally extended conduct?

In Bratman, consciousness is most important as reflective practical access and self-governing standpoint. The agent can treat certain matters as settled, recognize implications of intentions, fill in plans, detect inconsistency, and guide conduct in light of policies. These are not necessarily all occurrent episodes of consciousness. Much plan-guided structure operates in the background. But the agent’s practical consciousness includes the capacity to bring commitments into deliberative view, assess them, and act from them.

So the extracted philosophy of consciousness is modest. Bratman helps explain consciousness as agency-consciousness, not consciousness as phenomenal appearing. He gives a theory of deliberative self-location: how a person’s mind can be organized around commitments that answer the question, “What am I doing?” or “Where do I stand?” But he does not explain why pain feels like pain, why perception has phenomenal character, or why consciousness exists at all.

That limitation should not be treated as a defect if one is extracting a philosophy of practical mind. Bratman’s achievement is not a complete theory of consciousness. It is a theory of minded agency, where some of the most important mental structures are not sensations but commitments.

11. Mind and normativity: consistency, coherence, and stability

Bratman’s planning theory is also a theory of practical rationality. Plans are subject to norms. If I intend an end, and I believe a certain means is necessary, rational pressure arises to intend the means or revise the end. If I intend incompatible actions, my intentions are defective. If I constantly abandon plans for trivial reasons, I lose diachronic stability.

The norms Bratman emphasizes are not merely external moral norms. They are internal norms of planning agency: consistency, means-end coherence, and stability over time. His later essays defend these plan-related norms as norms of practical rationality, appealing both to the pragmatic support they provide and to their role in self-governance.

This is important because Bratman’s mind is not only causal but normative. Plans cause and guide action, but they also make demands. A planning agent can be irrational in distinctive ways: by intending incompatible things, by refusing necessary means, by constantly reconsidering, by failing to integrate policies, or by letting local impulses defeat settled commitments without sufficient reason.

The Portuguese reception brings out one important nuance. Lima Rui stresses that, for Bratman, intentions and plans shape the deliberative background; they help determine options and structure future conduct, but they should not simply be treated as ordinary reasons weighed alongside desires and beliefs. This protects Bratman from a “bootstrapping” problem: merely intending something should not automatically manufacture a substantive reason to do it. Rather, intentions structure deliberation in ways that are rationally significant without being magical sources of value.

This point matters theologically too. A vow, plan, or commitment can change what practical questions arise for me, but it does not by itself make the end good. A person can be excellently self-governed in the pursuit of a bad end. Bratman’s theory of agency therefore needs supplementation by ethics, theology, or value theory if one wants a full doctrine of good action.

12. Autonomy without value perfection

Bratman does not identify autonomy with doing what is morally best or with acting on correct evaluative judgments. In Structures of Agency, he resists the idea that agential authority is grounded simply in the content of value judgments. He instead emphasizes the role played by intention-like commitments, policies, and their place in the agent’s practical organization.

This gives him a formal account of autonomy. An action can be self-governed because it flows from the agent’s authoritative practical standpoint, even if the standpoint is morally flawed. That is both a strength and a limitation. It is a strength because it explains how bad people can still act intentionally, autonomously, and responsibly. It is a limitation because it cannot by itself distinguish sanctity from depravity, wisdom from folly, or virtue from vice.

The Spanish-language article “Bratman, autonomía y autogobierno” captures this issue well. It describes Bratman’s self-governance model as hierarchical, tied to the agent’s psychological functioning, higher-order policies, rational demands of consistency, coherence, and stability over time. But it also raises a critique: autonomous agency may exist without self-governing policies, and there may be tension between autonomy understood as temporally extended agency and autonomy understood through rational demands.

That critique is philosophically serious. Does self-governance require higher-order policies? Can a spontaneous or less reflective agent be autonomous? Does Bratman over-intellectualize agency by treating planning structures as central? Bratman himself is careful: he does not say all agents are planning agents. In “Modest Sociality and the Distinctiveness of Intention,” he says that not every agent is a planning agent, though adult humans in broadly modern contexts typically are.

That qualification matters for theology, anthropology, and philosophy of mind. Children, nonhuman animals, severely cognitively impaired persons, mystics, impulsive agents, and agents in ritual or ecstatic contexts may not fit Bratman’s paradigmatic planning model neatly. Bratman’s theory is strongest for mature, temporally extended, reflective, socially embedded human agency.

13. Shared agency: the social extension of the planning mind

Bratman’s philosophy of mind becomes social through his theory of shared agency. He asks how individual planning structures can support small-scale acting together: singing a duet, dancing together, painting a house, conversing, or walking together. In Shared Agency, he explicitly connects this project to the earlier question from Intention, Plans, and Practical Reason: what conception of mind and rational agency emerges when future-directed intentions and plans are taken seriously?

His answer is not that a new group mind appears. Nor is it that shared agency reduces to isolated individual intentions. Bratman defends what he calls, in effect, a middle path: shared intention is built from individual planning states plus special interrelations among them. The participants’ plans must mesh, they must know enough about one another’s intentions, and they must be mutually responsive in ways that support coordinated action.

The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes Bratman’s account as one that analyzes shared intention in terms of individual participants and the interrelations among their attitudes, without positing a plural agent. Shared intentions coordinate personal plans and intentional actions and provide a background for bargaining and negotiation.

This has direct bearing on “collective consciousness” and “group soul.” Bratman’s view allows robust shared agency without a literal group subject of consciousness. There is no need to posit a collective mind over and above the minds of the participants. But neither is the social level dismissed as an illusion. Shared agency is real because the individual planning states are interlocked in distinctive ways.

14. Against both atomism and mystical collectivism

Bratman’s position on social mind is subtle enough that it is easy to misread. It is individualist in the sense that intentions belong to individuals. But it is not atomistic in the sense of treating each individual’s intention as isolated. Shared agency depends on relational structures among individuals’ plans.

The German reception makes this point clearly. A German article on “Geteilte Absichten” describes Bratman’s approach as individualistic but non-atomistic: shared intentions require not only meshing subplans but also reciprocal relations among individual attitudes, such that the structure is more than a mere cognitive connection.

The French discussion of collective intentionality also helps clarify the issue. One French source presents debates about whether shared intentions have a bearer or subject, and it treats Bratman as important for a relational account that avoids both an individualistic reduction and a super-agent model. The same discussion raises difficult questions about collective experience, using religious examples involving Catholics and the German Catholic Church after Pope Benedict.

The Spanish legal-philosophical reception makes the theological and metaphysical stakes especially vivid. One Spanish article notes that if collective intention is treated as a mental state, one might be tempted either to posit an implausible collective mind—associated there with Hegelian images such as the spirit of nations—or to explain collective intention through linked individual mental states. It then presents Bratman’s account of shared intentions as a way of explaining collective action through distinctive individual intentions, commitment, meshing subplans, and mutual recognition of participants as intentional agents.

This is a valuable non-Anglophone contribution to the report because it connects Bratman’s theory to the metaphysics of collective mind. For theological contexts—church, liturgy, congregation, peoplehood, covenant community—Bratman gives a way to speak of communal agency without positing a literal communal soul or group consciousness.

15. Institutional agency and religious congregations

Bratman’s later work extends the planning model from small-scale shared agency to institutional agency. Shared and Institutional Agency argues that human lives involve diachronic organization, small-scale shared action, and institutions, and that the key is the capacity for planning agency. The Stanford/OUP page explicitly lists a religious congregation among the kinds of institutions relevant to this discussion.

Another OUP chapter description speaks of “mind-shaped practical organization” in individual diachronic activity, small-scale social organization, and institutions, including neighborhood associations, clubs, religious congregations, legal systems, and democratic states.

This is the most direct point of contact with theology in Bratman’s published philosophical orbit. He is not offering ecclesiology, but his institutional theory can illuminate ecclesial agency. A religious congregation can be understood as an institution whose members’ plans, roles, norms, expectations, and shared practical structures allow it to act, persist, deliberate, worship, govern, and maintain identity over time.

This is not the same as saying the congregation has a soul. A Bratmanian analysis would resist that metaphysical leap. But it would also resist the idea that the congregation is merely a heap of individuals. The congregation’s agency is constituted by structured interrelations among members, roles, policies, norms, and institutional plans. That is philosophically rich for theology because many religious communities do speak as if “the church acts,” “the congregation repents,” “the community remembers,” or “the people of God promises.” Bratman gives tools for analyzing such claims without either flattening them into individual psychology or inflating them into literal group consciousness.

16. Bratman, Searle, and Gilbert: three ways to think social mind

Bratman’s theory is often contrasted with John Searle and Margaret Gilbert. Searle argues for a primitive form of “we-intention” that is not reducible to individual “I-intentions.” Gilbert argues for joint commitment and plural subjects, often with stronger normativity built into shared agency. Bratman takes a more modest route.

In Shared Agency, Bratman contrasts his account with Searle’s appeal to a special we-intention and Gilbert’s joint-commitment approach. He argues that modest sociality can be explained by structures continuous with individual planning agency, together with cognitive structures such as common knowledge.

The Portuguese comparison between Bratman and Searle is useful here. Motta notes that, unlike Searle’s broader theory of intentionality, Bratman focuses more specifically on intention and its ontology, rather than on desire, belief, hope, fear, and other intentional states in general.

This contrast matters for philosophy of mind. Bratman does not give a general theory of intentionality as aboutness. He gives a theory of intention as commitment and planning structure. That is narrower than Searle’s philosophy of mind, but much more precise for explaining agency, self-governance, and shared action.

17. The theological significance of Bratman’s anti-homuncular self

Theologically, the most important negative result is this: Bratman’s self is not a separate internal substance that governs the psyche from outside. He explicitly rejects a picture of self-governance as the operation of a separate self pulling strings within the psychic economy.

For substance-dualist theology, this is a challenge. If “soul” means an immaterial substance that literally stands behind or above psychological processes as their metaphysical owner, Bratman offers no support for that doctrine. His framework is naturalistic and functional. Indeed, in Shared Agency he says that moving from a desire-belief model to a planning-structure model need not introduce a new obstacle to locating agency in the natural order.

But for theological anthropology more broadly, Bratman is not hostile or irrelevant. Many theological accounts of soul do not treat it merely as a ghostly substance. They also use “soul” to name the person’s practical unity, moral orientation, will, character, fidelity, and relation to a community. On those meanings, Bratman is highly relevant.

He can help distinguish several theological questions that are often fused. Does the person have an immaterial soul? Bratman does not answer that affirmatively. Does the person have a practical unity deeper than momentary impulse? Yes, Bratman gives a powerful account of that. Can the will be stable or fragmented? Yes. Can communities act without having literal group souls? Yes. Can vows, promises, disciplines, and institutions structure agency over time? Yes. Does self-governance require substantive moral or divine goods? Bratman’s theory alone does not say so; it must be supplemented.

18. Bratmanian soul as will, character, and policy

If we translate Bratman into older moral-theological language, intention corresponds partly to will, plans correspond partly to rule of life, policies correspond partly to character-forming commitments, and self-governance corresponds partly to practical integrity.

This translation must be handled carefully. Bratman is not simply restating Augustine, Aquinas, Calvin, Kierkegaard, or any other theologian in analytic vocabulary. His project is secular and action-theoretic. But his concepts map onto traditional concerns. The “soul” as seat of action is not a mysterious inner object; it is the living organization of the person’s commitments.

A Bratmanian account of spiritual discipline might say: spiritual practices alter the structure of practical deliberation by embedding stable policies and plans. A prayer rule, fasting discipline, liturgical calendar, or vow of service does not merely create isolated desires. It changes what is treated as settled, what later options are filtered out, what means-end questions arise, and how the agent coordinates present conduct with long-term identity.

A Bratmanian account of sin might say: sin is not only choosing a bad act. It may also be the corruption of the planning standpoint, the adoption of policies that organize life around defective ends, or the fragmentation of agency under temptation. Conversely, repentance is not just regret; it is reorientation of planning structures.

A Bratmanian account of virtue might say: virtue involves not merely good impulses, but stable policies and commitments that structure future deliberation. This is not a complete virtue ethics, because Bratman does not supply the substantive account of the good. But it is a powerful formal anatomy of practical formation.

19. The mind as not merely conscious: background commitments

A tempting but mistaken reading would identify Bratman’s planning agency with explicit conscious deliberation. Bratman’s agents deliberate, but plans often function in the background. Prior intentions filter options without being constantly rehearsed. A stable plan can guide conduct precisely because the agent need not consciously reopen it at every moment.

This is philosophically important. Bratman’s mind includes standing states, not just occurrent experiences. An intention can be real while not currently in focal consciousness. A person can be guided by a policy or plan even when attending to details of execution rather than to the policy itself.

That means Bratman’s theory helps bridge conscious and non-occurrent mentality. Practical consciousness is not exhausted by what is presently before the mind. It includes the agent’s standing commitments and policies insofar as they shape reasoning, action, and self-governance.

This is also why Bratman is relevant to debates about character, habit, and formation. Habits are not identical to plans, and Bratman is not primarily a habit theorist. But standing plans and policies can become part of the stable background through which the agent acts. They are less fleeting than occurrent desire and more practically structured than mere disposition.

20. The Bratmanian person: neither pure spontaneity nor mechanical program

Bratman’s agent is neither a pure spontaneous chooser nor a mechanical automaton. The agent is not free because she deliberates without causal antecedents. Bratman is comfortable with causally embedded agency. But the agent is also not merely pushed around by desires. She is organized by commitments that have rational and functional roles.

This yields a compatibilist-friendly philosophy of mind. Bratman’s theory can fit with a natural order while preserving robust agency, self-governance, and responsibility. The Italian reception’s emphasis on Bratman defending the role of the agent within an event-causal theory of action is exactly right.

This matters for theology because many theological accounts struggle with the relation between divine providence, natural causation, and human agency. Bratman does not address providence, but he provides an account of human agency that does not require contra-causal metaphysics. The will is not an uncaused spark; it is a structured, causally embedded, norm-governed practical organization.

Whether that is sufficient for a given theological doctrine of freedom is a further question. A libertarian theologian may find Bratman insufficient. A compatibilist theologian may find him congenial. A virtue theologian may find him formally illuminating but ethically incomplete.

21. What Bratman does not provide

A rigorous extraction must mark the limits of the source.

First, Bratman does not provide a theory of phenomenal consciousness. He does not explain qualia, subjectivity, perceptual presence, bodily feeling, or the hard problem.

Second, he does not provide a doctrine of the immortal soul, resurrection, afterlife, divine image, grace, sanctification, or spiritual experience. Theological use of Bratman requires constructive supplementation.

Third, he does not provide a full theory of emotion. Emotions can affect planning, stability, temptation, and self-governance, but they are not the central object of his account.

Fourth, he does not provide a full phenomenology of embodiment. His examples are embodied, but his theoretical emphasis falls on planning structures, intentions, and norms.

Fifth, he does not treat all human beings as planning agents in the same way. He explicitly allows that not all agents are planning agents, while adult humans in broadly modern contexts typically are. This leaves open difficult questions about infants, animals, disability, dementia, trauma, altered states, and ritual forms of agency.

Sixth, he does not by himself determine what ends are worthy. His theory can analyze coherent evil as well as coherent good. Ethical and theological theories must supply standards of value.

These limitations do not make Bratman shallow. They define his domain. He is giving a deep account of practical mind, not a total metaphysics of spirit.

22. The non-English reception: what it adds

The non-English materials are not merely decorative. They highlight interpretive possibilities less prominent in a purely Anglo-American reconstruction.

The Spanish reception foregrounds autonomy and collective mind. Ribeiro’s Spanish-language discussion of Bratman on autonomy and self-governance emphasizes the hierarchical structure of self-governing agency, higher-order policies, and the rational demands of consistency, coherence, and stability, while pressing the objection that autonomous agency may exist without such policies. The Spanish legal-philosophical discussion of collective action highlights the metaphysical problem of collective intention: either one posits a questionable collective mind, or one explains collective intention through linked individual mental states. It uses Bratman’s theory to pursue the second path.

The French reception foregrounds bearerhood, relationality, and collective experience. The OpenEdition discussion of collective intentionality treats Bratman as part of a debate about whether shared intentions require a subject or bearer and explores relational accounts that avoid both isolated individualism and a super-agent. It also raises religious examples involving Catholic collective identity, which are directly relevant to theological readings of group agency.

The German reception foregrounds non-atomistic individualism. The German article “Geteilte Absichten” stresses that Bratman’s account is individualistic but not atomistic: shared intentions require reciprocal relations among individual attitudes and meshing subplans. A German dissertation’s reconstruction of Bratman’s conditions for shared activity likewise emphasizes interlocking intentions, stability, minimal cooperation, and mutual responsiveness.

The Italian reception foregrounds agency under pressure from naturalism and modern cognitive science. Ginocchietti and Verrucci both present Bratman as a philosopher of strong agency, self-governance, planning, and diachronic continuity, with special relevance to worries that causal explanation, neuroscience, or social psychology might dissolve the agent.

The Portuguese reception foregrounds the ontology and deliberative function of intention. Lima Rui’s discussion stresses that intentions and plans coordinate future conduct and social activity, impose consistency and means-end coherence demands, and function as deliberative background rather than ordinary reasons. Motta’s comparison of Bratman and Searle emphasizes that Bratman’s theory is more narrowly focused on intention and its ontology than Searle’s wider theory of intentionality.

Together, these non-English sources reinforce a broader conclusion: Bratman is most fruitful when read not just as a philosopher of “intention” in a narrow sense, but as a theorist of practical unity, agency over time, and the metaphysics of shared action.

23. Extracted comprehensive philosophy of mind

The philosophy of mind one can extract from Bratman has the following structure.

The mind is practical before it is merely contemplative. Bratman’s central mental states are not sensations but intentions, plans, policies, and practical standpoints. The mind is the organized capacity to settle, guide, revise, and coordinate action.

The mind is diachronic. A person’s mentality is not exhausted by present experience. Prior intentions and plans structure future deliberation and action. The self is extended through time by practical continuity.

The mind is resource-bounded. Human rationality is finite. Plans make rational agency possible by limiting deliberative overload. They reduce the need to reconsider everything from scratch.

The mind is norm-governed. Planning agency is subject to demands of consistency, means-end coherence, and reasonable stability. These norms are internal to the functioning of planning agency.

The mind is anti-homuncular. There is no need for a separate inner self that governs the psychological economy from outside. Self-governance is a structure within that economy.

The will is intention-like commitment. Will is not a metaphysical substance or a bare power of choice. It is the agent’s practical organization through commitments, policies, and plans.

The self is a practical standpoint. It is not merely a bundle of desires, nor a Cartesian ego. It is the organized standpoint from which the agent acts, evaluates, persists, and governs.

Consciousness is practical and reflective, but not fully theorized phenomenologically. Bratman helps explain what it is to know where one stands and act from commitments. He does not explain phenomenal subjectivity.

Social mind is interlocking planning agency, not literal group consciousness. Shared intentions are built from individual intentions and their relations. Institutions scale up these structures through roles, norms, and policies.

The soul, if reconstructed, is practical unity. Bratman does not defend an immaterial soul. But his theory can reinterpret the soul as the diachronic organization of will, commitment, self-governance, and shared life.

24. Bratman and theology: careful constructive use

Theologically, Bratman should be used neither dismissively nor naively. He does not give theology, but he gives tools theology can use.

For doctrines of the will, Bratman clarifies how commitments differ from desires. This is useful for analyzing temptation, vow, obedience, discipline, and perseverance.

For doctrines of sin, Bratman clarifies how agency can be fragmented. Sin may be understood not only as isolated wrongful choice but also as disordered practical organization.

For doctrines of virtue and sanctification, Bratman clarifies how stable policies and plans can shape conduct over time. Formation is not merely affective; it is also structural.

For ecclesiology, Bratman clarifies how communities can act without requiring a literal group soul. Religious congregations, churches, and institutions can be treated as organized structures of shared and institutional agency.

For theological anthropology, Bratman offers a secular formal account of practical personhood. But theology must still supply what Bratman does not: the metaphysics of creation, divine image, grace, resurrection, ultimate goods, and the relation between embodied personhood and God.

For debates about soul, Bratman’s usefulness depends on what “soul” means. If soul means immortal immaterial substance, Bratman is largely silent and methodologically unsupportive. If soul means the person’s living unity of will, character, and practical orientation, Bratman is profoundly relevant.

25. Final assessment

Bratman’s implicit philosophy of mind is one of the most important contemporary accounts of the practical mind. Its center is not consciousness as raw experience but agency as temporally extended, plan-shaped, norm-governed practical organization. He shows how intentions are distinctive mental states, how plans structure deliberation, how self-governance can be real without a homuncular self, and how shared agency can be real without a literal group mind.

The Bratmanian mind is a planning mind. The Bratmanian self is a practical standpoint. The Bratmanian will is a system of intention-like commitments. The Bratmanian soul, if that term is allowed, is not a ghostly substance but the diachronic integrity of a self-governing practical life. The Bratmanian community is not a collective consciousness but an interlocking structure of individual planning agents whose intentions, subplans, roles, and norms make shared and institutional action possible.

The result is not a complete philosophy of consciousness, and it is not a theology of the soul. But it is a rigorous philosophical anatomy of the structures that many traditions have associated with soul: will, integrity, commitment, self-command, fidelity, practical identity, and participation in common life. Its theological value lies precisely there. Bratman does not tell us what the soul ultimately is before God. He tells us, with unusual precision, how a finite minded agent can govern itself over time, and how such agents can act together.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
